title: 1 Kings 15
subtitle: Abijah and Asa in Judah; Nadab and Baasha in Israel

sections.header: Verses 1–24 — Asa's Reforms: He Did Right in the Eyes of the LORD

heb.paragraph: Asa's heart wasšālēm(fully devoted, v.14) "all his days" — the same word used negatively of Solomon (11:4). What Solomon failed to maintain, Asa achieves. He is the first king evaluated positively since the division, restoring hope for the Davidic line.

refs.note: "Asa's heart was fully committed to the LORD all his life." The Chronicler's parallel evaluation is identical.
The Chronicler adds the prophet Hanani's rebuke for the Ben-Hadad alliance: "You relied on the king of Aram and not on the LORD." The political alliance was a failure of faith.

notes.note: Calvin: Asa's deposition of his own grandmother teaches that family loyalty must yield to loyalty to God. When relatives promote idolatry, the faithful must choose God over kinship. This is costly but non-negotiable.

Calvin: The high places remained — even the best reformer's work is incomplete in this life. Let the church not despair of imperfect reform. God honours sincere effort even when the result falls short of perfection.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note:mipliṣet(horrid/obscene thing) — the object Maakah made for Asherah. The term implies something repulsive, possibly a phallic cult image. Asa burned it in the Kidron Valley — the same valley where Josiah later destroyed cult objects (2 Kgs 23:4, 6).

NET Note:raq habbāmôt lōʾ sārû(only the high places were not removed) — theraq(only/except) particle is the narrator's trademark qualifying device, appearing in positive evaluations of both Solomon (3:3) and now Asa.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Wiseman: Ben-Hadad I of Damascus (c.900–860 BC) is attested in the Melqart Stele discovered near Aleppo (1939). The Aramean kingdom of Damascus was the dominant regional power in the 9th century, making it a natural ally for Judah against Israel.

Wiseman: Ben-Hadad's conquest of Ijon, Dan, and Abel Beth Maakah in northern Israel is consistent with Aramean expansion southward from the Anti-Lebanon region. Archaeological evidence at Dan shows destruction layers datable to this period.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Provan: Asa is the narrator's first ray of hope in the southern kingdom. After Rehoboam and Abijah, a king arises who "did right." The Deuteronomistic historian uses Asa to demonstrate that the pattern of decline is not inevitable — reform is possible when the king's heart is right.

Provan: The "high places not removed" caveat follows Asa into his positive evaluation. This persistent qualifier demonstrates the narrator's honesty: even the best Judahite kings fall short of the Deuteronomic ideal. Complete reform awaits Josiah — and even he cannot save the nation.

hist.context: Abijah reigns briefly (3 years) and "committed all the sins his father had done." Then Asa — the first reformer king — reigns 41 years. He expels the male shrine prostitutes, removes the idols, and even deposes his grandmother Maakah for her Asherah pole. His heart is "fully devoted" — but with a caveat: "the high places were not removed." The narrator's "except" returns (cf. 3:3). Asa is faithful but not completely so. He also strips the temple treasury to buy an alliance with Ben-Hadad of Aram against Israel — using sacred resources for political strategy.

notes.note: Every genuine revival in Israel's history was rooted in a return to God's Word. The pattern is consistent: rediscovery of Scripture leads to repentance, which leads to reformation of worship. This remains the pattern for spiritual renewal in every age — the church is reformed and revived only as it returns to the authority of God's written Word.

True spiritual reformation always involves both negative and positive elements: tearing down what is false and building up what is true. Half-hearted reform — removing some idols while tolerating others — never produces lasting change. God calls His people to wholehearted devotion, not partial obedience (Deut 6:5; Matt 22:37).

Every revival recorded in Scripture was initiated by God and sustained by obedience to His Word. The pattern is instructive for the modern church: spiritual renewal does not come through innovative programs or cultural accommodation but through humble return to the unchanging truth of Scripture. 'Will you not revive us again, that your people may rejoice in you?' (Ps 85:6).

sections.header: Verses 25–34 — Baasha Destroys Jeroboam's House; Walks in His Sin

heb.gloss: in his sin which he caused Israel to sin
heb.paragraph: The formula "walked in the way of Jeroboam and in his sin, which he caused Israel to sin" (v.34) becomes the standard verdict for every northern king. It appears over 20 times in Kings. The golden calves are the measuring stick against which every reign is evaluated.

refs.note: Ahijah's prophecy against Jeroboam's house — fulfilled by Baasha in v.29.
Jehu the prophet will deliver an identical prophecy against Baasha — the destroyer becomes the destroyed.

notes.note: Calvin: God uses Baasha — a brutal usurper — to fulfil his prophetic word against Jeroboam. This does not sanctify Baasha's violence; it demonstrates God's sovereignty over even sinful human actions. The instrument of judgment is itself judged (ch. 16).

Calvin: Baasha repeats Jeroboam's sin after witnessing Jeroboam's destruction. How blind is the human heart that it cannot learn even from the ruin of others! The examples of judgment that should produce fear produce only continued rebellion.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note:kĕḏabar YHWH ʾăšer dibbēr bĕyad ʿabdô ʾĂḥiyyāh(according to the word of the LORD which he spoke by the hand of his servant Ahijah) — the fulfilment formula explicitly connects event to prophecy. "By the hand of" (bĕyad) is the standard OT idiom for prophetic mediation.

NET Note: The formulaic evaluation "he walked in the way of Jeroboam and in his sin which he caused Israel to sin" (wayyēlek bĕderek Yārŏbʿām ûbĕḥaṭṭāʾtô) uses the construct chain to bind the king's personal sin to its national effects. Every northern king inherits Jeroboam's guilt by perpetuating his system.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Wiseman: Gibbethon (Tell el-Melat, near Gezer) was a Levitical city (Josh 21:23) in Philistine territory. Israel's siege there — interrupted by the coup — shows ongoing border conflict with the Philistines. The location reappears in 16:15–17 when Zimri's coup also occurs during a Gibbethon siege.

Wiseman: The complete destruction of a predecessor's dynasty was standard practice in ANE succession by coup. Assyrian, Babylonian, and Hittite records document similar dynastic purges. The practice eliminated rival claimants but perpetuated cycles of violence.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Provan: The narrator notes the fulfilment of Ahijah's prophecy (14:10) — maintaining the theme that prophetic word drives historical outcome. Every major event in Kings is traced to a prior prophetic announcement. The narrative is not random history but executed prophecy.

Provan: Baasha's identical sin demonstrates the narrator's point: the problem in Israel is not personal but systemic. Change the king, keep the calves, and nothing changes. Structural reform — not just leadership change — is what Israel needs and never gets.

hist.context: Nadab reigns only 2 years before Baasha assassinates him and destroys Jeroboam's entire house — fulfilling Ahijah's prophecy (14:10). But Baasha then walks in the same sin: the golden calves remain. The pattern is established: northern kings rise by violence, destroy their predecessors, but perpetuate the same apostasy. The mechanism changes; the sin does not. The narrator demonstrates that the problem is not the dynasty but the system — Jeroboam's calves corrupt whoever rules, regardless of how they gained power.

notes.note: The devastating judgment recorded here was not arbitrary divine wrath but the long-warned consequence of persistent covenant violation. God had patiently sent prophet after prophet (2 Chr 36:15-16), but the people refused to repent. Believers must learn that God's patience, though immense, is not unlimited — He will discipline His people to restore them (Heb 12:5-11).

The theological framework of Kings evaluates every monarch by a single standard: fidelity to the covenant expressed in exclusive worship of Yahweh at the chosen place. Political success, military prowess, and economic prosperity are irrelevant apart from covenant obedience. This standard anticipates the NT principle that faithfulness to God's Word is the only measure of true success (Rev 2:10).

The judgment of exile was not the end of God's purposes but a severe mercy designed to purify and restore. God disciplines those He loves (Heb 12:6). For the believer, seasons of divine discipline are not evidence of God's abandonment but of His fatherly care. He prunes every branch that bears fruit so that it will bear even more (John 15:2).

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVNevertheless, for David\u2019s sake the Lord his God gave him a lamp in Jerusalem by raising up a son to succeed him and by making Jerusalem strong.ESVNevertheless, for David\u2019s sake the LORD his God gave him a lamp in Jerusalem, setting up his son after him, and establishing Jerusalem,NIVHe committed all the sins his father had done before him; his heart was not fully devoted to the Lord his God, as the heart of David his forefather had been.ESVAnd he walked in all the sins that his father did before him, and his heart was not wholly true to the LORD his God, as the heart of David his father.
